80	\section{The morphism to \(\mathbb P^1\) from two global sections}
81	\begin{lemma}
82	
83	
84	\leanok
85	[Morphism to \(\mathbb P^1\) from a globally generating pair of sections]
86	  \label{lem:morphism_to_p1_from_global_sections}
87	  
88	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.morphismToP1OfGlobalSections}
89	  Let \(X\) be a scheme over a field \(\bar k\), let \(\mathcal L\) be an invertible
90	  sheaf on \(X\), and let \(s_0, s_1 \in H^0(X, \mathcal L)\) be two global
91	  sections whose common zero locus is empty:
92	  \[
93	    \{x \in X : s_0(x) = 0 \text{ and } s_1(x) = 0\} \;=\; \emptyset.
94	  \]
95	  (Equivalently, \(s_0\) and \(s_1\) generate \(\mathcal L\) at every point.) Then
96	  there exists a unique morphism of \(\bar k\)-schemes
97	  \[
98	    \varphi_{(s_0, s_1)} \colon X \longrightarrow \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}
99	  \]
100	  whose pullback of \(\mathcal O_{\mathbb P^1}(1)\) is \(\mathcal L\) and whose
101	  pullbacks of the standard coordinate sections $x_0, x_1 \in
102	  H^0(\mathbb P^1, \mathcal O(1))\( are \)s_0\( and \)s_1$ respectively. On the
103	  basic-open chart $D_+(x_0) = \mathbb A^1_{\bar k} \subset
104	  \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\( the morphism \)\varphi_{(s_0, s_1)}\( is the map ``\)x
105	  \mapsto s_1(x) / s_0(x)\(''; on the chart \)D_+(x_1)\( it is the map ``\)x
106	  \mapsto s_0(x) / s_1(x)$''; the two definitions agree on the overlap
107	  \(D_+(x_0) \cap D_+(x_1) = \mathbb G_{m, \bar k}\) by the cocycle identity
108	  \((s_1 / s_0) \cdot (s_0 / s_1) = 1\).
109	\end{lemma}
110	\begin{proof}
111	
112	
113	
114	\leanok
115	  This is the standard linear-system construction in the form of
116	  Hartshorne's Theorem~II.7.1 (``Morphisms to \(\mathbb P^n\)''), specialised to
117	  \(n = 1\). Mathlib (snapshot \texttt{b80f227}) ships the corresponding API
118	  under the name
119	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} in
120	  \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.ProjectiveSpectrum.Basic}:
121	  \begin{quote}
122	  \small
123	  \texttt{def Proj.fromOfGlobalSections \{\(\sigma\) A\} [CommRing A]
124	    [SetLike \(\sigma\) A] [AddSubgroupClass \(\sigma\) A] (\(\mathcal A\) :
125	    \(\mathbb N\) → \(\sigma\)) [GradedRing \(\mathcal A\)] \{X : Scheme\}\\
126	    \phantom{}\quad(f : A →+* X.presheaf.obj \(\top\))\\
127	    \phantom{}\quad(hf : Ideal.map f (HomogeneousIdeal.irrelevant \(\mathcal A\)).toIdeal = \(\top\)) :\\
128	    \phantom{}\quad(X \(\longrightarrow\) Proj \(\mathcal A\))}
129	  \end{quote}
130	  Specialising to \(\mathcal A = \bar k[x_0, x_1]\) with the standard $\mathbb
131	  N\(-grading by total degree, the inputs are a graded \)\bar k$-algebra
132	  homomorphism \(f \colon \bar k[x_0, x_1] \to H^0(X, \mathcal O_X)\) and a
133	  proof that the irrelevant ideal \((x_0, x_1) \cdot \bar k[x_0, x_1]\) maps to
134	  the unit ideal of \(H^0(X, \mathcal O_X)\) (i.e.\ no common zeros). The
135	  resulting morphism lands in $\mathrm{Proj}\,\bar k[x_0, x_1] = \mathbb
136	  P^1_{\bar k}\( (Hartshorne~II, Example~7.1.3, the construction of \)\mathbb
137	  P^n\( as a \)\mathrm{Proj}\(). When \)\mathcal L$ is non-trivial the present
138	  lemma's signature is reduced to the Mathlib API by working on the open
139	  cover \(X = D(s_0) \cup D(s_1)\), where on each chart the sections become
140	  honest global functions (\(s_1 / s_0\) on \(D(s_0)\), \(s_0 / s_1\) on \(D(s_1)\),
141	  agreeing on the overlap). The chart-glue is performed by the
142	  project-bespoke wrapper
143	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.morphismToP1OfGlobalSections}, whose body
144	  in \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RationalCurveIso.lean} chains
145	  \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} with the chart-restriction-and-glue
146	  pattern of \texttt{Scheme.Cover.glueMorphisms}.
147	  Uniqueness follows from the standard separation argument for morphisms to
148	  a separated scheme: two morphisms \(\varphi, \psi \colon X \to \mathbb P^1\)
149	  with \(\varphi^* x_i = \psi^* x_i\) for \(i = 0, 1\) agree on the basic-open
150	  charts \(D_+(x_0), D_+(x_1)\) (where they pull back the affine coordinates to
151	  the same rational function), and these charts cover \(\mathbb P^1\), so the
152	  two morphisms agree globally by gluing.
153	\end{proof}
154	\section{Degree of a morphism to \(\mathbb P^1\) via its pole divisor}
155	% NOTE (iter-190 plan-phase Pin 2 corrective; supersedes iter-182 narrative).
156	% `Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor \varphi` is the Weil divisor \(\varphi^*[\infty]\),
157	% defined as `positivePart (principal (\varphi^{\#} t_\infty))` in Lean.
158	% The supporting `Scheme.WeilDivisor.positivePart` def lives in
159	% `RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex` (\ref{def:WeilDivisor_positivePart}); the
160	% Hartshorne~II.6.9 identity that powers the existential degree statement
161	% lives there as \ref{lem:degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank}.
162	\begin{lemma}
163	
164	
165	\leanok
166	[Degree of a non-constant morphism to \(\mathbb P^1\) via the preimage of \(\infty\)]
167	  \label{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor}
168	  \uses{def:WeilDivisor_positivePart,
169	        lem:degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank, def:principal_divisor,
170	        def:divisor_degree, thm:principal_deg_zero}
171	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.morphism_degree_via_pole_divisor}
172	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV \S2, opening paragraph of "Hurwitz's Theorem"
173	  % (definition of the degree of a finite morphism of curves) (read from
174	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 316, doc p.~299).
175	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Recall that the \emph{degree} of a finite morphism
176	  % \(f: X \to Y\) of curves is defined to be the degree \([K(X):K(Y)]\) of the
177	  % extension of function fields (II, \S 6)."
178	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.2, p.~299, opening of \S2.}
179	  Let \(\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) be a non-constant morphism
180	  from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\). Then
181	  \(\varphi\) is finite, its function-field extension
182	  \(\bar k(\mathbb P^1) \hookrightarrow K(C)\) (the canonical
183	  \(\varphi\)-induced map, supplied as an
184	  \texttt{[Algebra (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).functionField C.functionField]}
185	  instance argument on the Lean side) is finite, and there exists a Weil
186	  divisor \(D \in \Div(C)\) such that
187	  \[
188	    D \;=\; \varphi^*[\infty]
189	    \quad\text{and}\quad
190	    \deg(D) \;=\; [\,K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)\,].
191	  \]
192	  Equivalently, the morphism \(\varphi\) satisfies
193	  \[
194	    [\,K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)\,] \;=\;
195	    \deg\!\left(\,\sum_{P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)}
196	                  \ord_P(\varphi^* t_\infty) \cdot [P]\,\right),
197	  \]
198	  where \(t_\infty \in K(\mathbb P^1)\) is a local parameter at \(\infty\)
199	  (equivalently \(t_\infty = 1/u\) for the standard affine coordinate \(u\) on
200	  \(\mathbb P^1 \setminus \{\infty\}\)); and for any closed point
201	  \(Q \in \mathbb P^1\) the same identification gives
202	  \(\deg(\varphi^*[Q]) = [K(C):\bar k(\mathbb P^1)]\).
203	  \emph{Convention on the pole divisor.} The Weil divisor
204	  \(\varphi^*[\infty]\) is exposed in Lean as
205	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor \(\varphi\)}, a
206	  noncomputable definition introduced (as a typed \texttt{sorry}) at the
207	  iter-182 plan-phase signature strengthening of this lemma. The body of
208	  \texttt{poleDivisor} is queued for iter-183+ closure via the standard
209	  affine-chart construction (\texttt{Ideal.sum\_ramification\_inertia}
210	  in Mathlib, applied at each closed point \(P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)\)
211	  to read off the local ramification multiplicity
212	  \(\ord_P(\varphi^* t_\infty)\) from the maximal ideal at \(\infty\) in
213	  \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\)). On the Lean side, the existential output of
214	  \texttt{morphism\_degree\_via\_pole\_divisor} packages
215	  \(D = \texttt{Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor}\;\varphi\) together with
216	  \(\deg(D) = \operatorname{Module.finrank}_{K(\mathbb P^1)} K(C)\),
217	  mirroring the OCofP pattern in which a typed-sorry def
218	  (\texttt{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField}) is introduced
219	  upstream of its consumer lemma.
220	\end{lemma}
221	% NOTE (iter-190 plan-phase, supersedes iter-189 review NOTE):
222	% Pin 2 corrective COMMITTED — Option (a). `Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor` is
223	% being refactored to return the *positive part* of the principal divisor

254	  \uses{def:WeilDivisor_positivePart, lem:degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank, def:principal_divisor, def:divisor_degree, thm:principal_deg_zero}
255	  \leanok
256	  A non-constant morphism between smooth proper curves over \(\bar k\) is
257	  automatically finite. Indeed, the image of \(\varphi\) is a closed
258	  irreducible subset of \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) that is not a point (else
259	  \(\varphi\) would be constant) and not empty, so it is all of $\mathbb
260	  P^1_{\bar k}\( (the only proper irreducible closed subset of \)\mathbb
261	  P^1_{\bar k}\( is a point); hence \)\varphi$ is surjective. A surjective
262	  morphism between smooth proper irreducible curves of equal dimension is
263	  finite (proper + quasi-finite, where quasi-finiteness comes from the
264	  fact that \(\varphi\) has zero-dimensional fibres: a positive-dimensional
265	  fibre would force a one-dimensional irreducible component in the fibre,
266	  contradicting irreducibility of \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) and \(C\) at
267	  generic points). Hence the function-field extension
268	  \(\bar k(\mathbb P^1) = \varphi^\# K(\mathbb P^1) \hookrightarrow K(C)\)
269	  is finite.
270	  The identification \(\deg(\varphi^*[Q]) = [K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)]\) for
271	  any closed point \(Q \in \mathbb P^1\) is the multiplicativity of the
272	  degree under finite pullback (Hartshorne~II.6.9): for a finite morphism
273	  \(\varphi \colon C \to C'\) of smooth proper curves and any divisor $D \in
274	  \Div(C')\(, \)\deg(\varphi^* D) = \deg(\varphi) \cdot \deg(D)$. Specialising
275	  to \(D = [Q]\) (a divisor of degree \(1\) since \(\kappa(Q) = \bar k\)) gives
276	  \(\deg(\varphi^*[Q]) = \deg(\varphi)\). Equivalently, the degree of
277	  \(\varphi\) equals the number of preimages of any closed point of $\mathbb
278	  P^1\(, counted with the ramification multiplicities \)\ord_P(\varphi^*
279	  t_\infty)\( at the preimage points \)P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)$.
280	  \emph{Sub-build note.} The lemma threads two auxiliary inputs:
281	  ``non-constant morphism between smooth proper curves is finite'' (the
282	  surjective-and-proper-and-quasi-finite argument above; Hartshorne~II.6.8)
283	  and ``multiplicativity of degree under finite pullback'' (Hartshorne~II.6.9
284	  and the multiplicativity of \(\deg\) under composition of \(\div\) and
285	  \(\deg\); the closely related ``\(\mathrm{div}(f)\) has degree zero'' specialisation
286	  is \texttt{thm:principal_deg_zero} of \texttt{RR.1}). Both are separable
287	  sub-lemmas inside \texttt{RR.4}'s Lean file
288	  (\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RationalCurveIso.lean}) and are
289	  slotted for closure in a follow-up iteration after the
290	  \texttt{morphism\_degree\_via\_pole\_divisor} skeleton lands.
291	\end{proof}

323	\begin{lemma}
324

327	[Degree-\(1\) morphism between smooth proper curves is an isomorphism]
328	  \label{lem:degree_one_morphism_iso}
329	  \uses{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor, def:functionFieldIso}
330	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.iso_of_degree_one}
331	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], I \S6 Corollary~6.12 (function-field-to-curve
332	  % equivalence of categories) (read from
333	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 62, doc p.~45)
334	  % + [Hartshorne], IV \S2 opening paragraph (degree of a finite morphism
335	  % of curves is the function-field extension degree) (read from
336	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 316, doc p.~299).
337	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Corollary 6.12. \emph{The following three categories are
338	  % equivalent:}
339	  % (i) \emph{nonsingular projective curves, and dominant morphisms;}
340	  % (ii) \emph{quasi-projective curves, and dominant rational maps;}
341	  % (iii) \emph{function fields of dimension 1 over \(k\), and \(k\)-homomorphisms.}"
342	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Recall that the \emph{degree} of a finite morphism
343	  % \(f: X \to Y\) of curves is defined to be the degree \([K(X):K(Y)]\) of the
344	  % extension of function fields (II, \S 6)."
345	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, I.6 Corollary~6.12 (p.~45) + IV.2 opening
346	  (p.~299).}
347	  Let \(C, C'\) be smooth proper geometrically irreducible curves over an
348	  algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), and let \(\varphi \colon C \to C'\) be
349	  a non-constant morphism whose induced function-field extension is of
350	  degree one,
351	  \[
352	    [\,K(C) : K(C')\,] \;=\; 1,
353	  \]
354	  formally
355	  \(\operatorname{Module.finrank}_{K(C')} K(C) = 1\) in the Lean signature.
356	  Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism of \(\bar k\)-schemes.
357	  \emph{Convention on the function-field map.} At each call-site of this
358	  lemma, the \(K(C')\)-algebra structure on \(K(C)\) used to interpret the
359	  degree \([K(C) : K(C')]\) is the canonical \(\varphi\)-induced
360	  function-field map, i.e.\ the composite
361	  \[
362	    K(C') \;\xrightarrow{\;\text{germ}\;}\;
363	    \mathcal O_{C', \varphi(\xi_C)}
364	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\varphi^\#\;}\;
365	    \mathcal O_{C, \xi_C}
366	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\text{frac.}\,\text{lift}\;}\; K(C)
367	  \]
368	  of the germ at the image of the generic point \(\xi_C \in C\) with the
369	  stalk-map of \(\varphi\) at \(\xi_C\) and the fraction-field lift at
370	  \(\xi_C\) (per \texttt{analogies/ratcurveiso-pin3.md}, Decision~2). On
371	  the Lean side this canonical instance is carried as a
372	  \texttt{[Algebra C'.functionField C.functionField]} instance argument
373	  to \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.iso\_of\_degree\_one}; consumers
374	  supply it explicitly at each call-site.

377	% from `Module.finrank = 1`) closed axiom-clean inline iter-189 (~10 LOC via

380	% via `Scheme.Hom.toNormalization`) remains a typed sorry in the body, with 4

383	\begin{proof}
384	
385	
386	
387	\leanok
388	  The function-field hypothesis \([K(C) : K(C')] = 1\) states that the
389	  canonical \(\varphi\)-induced map \(\varphi^\# \colon K(C') \to K(C)\) is a
390	  finite extension of degree one; since \(K(C')\) is a field and the
391	  extension has dimension one as a \(K(C')\)-vector space, \(\varphi^\#\) is
392	  an isomorphism of function fields \(K(C') \cong K(C)\). (Equivalently, by
393	  the IV.2-opening identification \(\deg(\varphi) = [K(C):K(C')]\), this is
394	  the statement \(\deg(\varphi) = 1\); the morphism \(\varphi\) is finite in
395	  this case by the standard argument that a non-constant morphism of
396	  smooth proper curves is finite, identical to the
397	  \texttt{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor} preamble.)
398	  By Hartshorne's Corollary~I.6.12, the assignment \(C \mapsto \bar k(C)\) is
399	  an equivalence of categories between smooth projective curves over $\bar
400	  k$ (with dominant morphisms) and function fields of transcendence degree
401	  one over \(\bar k\) (with \(\bar k\)-homomorphisms). Under this equivalence,
402	  the isomorphism \(\bar k(C') \cong \bar k(C)\) produced by the previous
403	  paragraph lifts to a unique isomorphism of smooth proper curves $C \to
404	  C'\(, and this isomorphism agrees with \)\varphi$ on the generic point (by
405	  construction: it is the inverse-image of \(\varphi^\#\) under the
406	  equivalence). The two morphisms \(C \to C'\) that agree on the generic
407	  point of the integral scheme \(C\) agree everywhere (a morphism out of an
408	  integral scheme to a separated scheme is determined by its restriction
409	  to the generic point, Hartshorne~II Ex.~4.2). Hence \(\varphi\) is itself
410	  the lift, and is therefore an isomorphism.
411	  \emph{Alternative direct argument.} Without invoking the
412	  equivalence-of-categories framing, the same conclusion is obtained
413	  scheme-theoretically: a finite degree-\(1\) morphism $\varphi \colon C \to
414	  C'$ between integral normal noetherian schemes is an isomorphism.
415	  Indeed, the direct image \(\varphi_* \mathcal O_C\) is a coherent
416	  \(\mathcal O_{C'}\)-module that is generically of rank $\deg(\varphi) =
417	  1\(, and an inclusion \)\mathcal O_{C'} \hookrightarrow \varphi_*
418	  \mathcal O_C\( of coherent rank-\)1$ sheaves whose generic ranks agree
419	  is an isomorphism over the locus where the quotient
420	  \(\varphi_* \mathcal O_C / \mathcal O_{C'}\) has trivial stalk. The
421	  quotient is a torsion coherent \(\mathcal O_{C'}\)-module of generic rank
422	  \(\deg(\varphi) - 1 = 0\), hence is itself torsion-free (a
423	  finitely-generated torsion module over a Dedekind domain of generic
424	  rank zero is zero), so vanishes. Therefore $\varphi_* \mathcal O_C =
425	  \mathcal O_{C'}\( and \)\varphi$ is the structure morphism of an
426	  isomorphism. Cf.\ Stacks tag~\textbf{0AVX} for the categorical version.
427	  \emph{Sub-build note.} The Lean signature
428	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.iso\_of\_degree\_one} consumes a
429	  non-constant morphism \(\varphi \colon C \to C'\) together with a
430	  \texttt{[Algebra C'.functionField C.functionField]} instance argument
431	  (the canonical \(\varphi\)-induced function-field map) and the
432	  function-field-extension-degree-one hypothesis
433	  \texttt{Module.finrank C'.functionField C.functionField = 1}, and
434	  produces an isomorphism \(C \cong C'\) in the slice category
435	  \(\mathtt{Over (Spec (.of\ kbar))}\). The proof body threads the Mathlib
436	  API \texttt{IsIntegralHom.iso\_of\_isUnit} (or the equivalent
437	  \texttt{IsAffineHom + Finite + RankOne} chain) and, where needed, the
438	  project-bespoke ``finite-morphism-of-degree-one is an isomorphism''
439	  helper. The Hartshorne~I.6.12 equivalence-of-categories framing is the
440	  intended high-level proof structure; the scheme-theoretic argument
441	  above is the spelled-out form for the Lean body.
442	\end{proof}
443	\section{Headline: genus-\(0\) curve over \(\bar k\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1\)}
444	\begin{theorem}
445	
446	\leanok
447	[Genus-\(0\) curve over \(\bar k\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1\)]
448	  \label{thm:genus_zero_curve_iso_p1}
449	  \uses{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero, cor:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_exists_nonconstant_genusZero,
450	        lem:morphism_to_p1_from_global_sections, lem:degree_via_pole_divisor,
451	        lem:degree_one_morphism_iso, def:genus}
452	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
453	  % already know that \(p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0\) (I, Ex. 7.2), so conversely
454	  % suppose given a curve \(X\) of genus 0. Let \(P, Q\) be two distinct
455	  % points of \(X\) and apply Riemann-Roch to the divisor \(D = P - Q\).
456	  % Since \(\deg(K - D) = -2\), using (1.3.3) above, we have \(l(K - D) = 0\),
457	  % and so we find \(l(D) = 1\). But \(D\) is a divisor of degree 0, so by
458	  % (1.2) we have \(D \sim 0\), in other words \(P \sim Q\). But this implies
459	  % \(X\) is rational (II, 6.10.1)."
460	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Example~1.3.5, p.~297.}
461	  Let \(\bar k\) be an algebraically closed field, and let \(C\) be a smooth
462	  proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\).
463	  Then there exists an isomorphism of \(\bar k\)-schemes
464	  \[
465	    \varphi \colon C \stackrel{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}.
466	  \]
467	  Concretely, the Lean signature
468	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1} (the existing pinned
469	  target at \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean:290}) is
470	  closed by the proof below; the existing interface block
471	  \texttt{chap:RiemannRoch_RationalCurveIso} of
472	  \texttt{chap:avr_for_rr} is its consumer-side restatement,
473	  pinning the same Lean declaration.
474	\end{theorem}
475	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the verbatim Example IV.1.3.5 text in the
476	% statement block above (Hartshorne states the classification and its
477	% proof inside the single Example; the proof is the second half beginning
478	% "We already know that \(p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0\) \ldots"). The present
479	% proof body restates Hartshorne's argument in the project's notation
480	% (linear-system construction \(C \to \mathbb P^1\) via $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C
481	% ([P]))\(, degree-\)1$ identification, isomorphism).
482	\begin{proof}
483	  Over the algebraically closed field \(\bar k\) the smooth proper
484	  geometrically irreducible curve \(C\) has closed points, all of which are
485	  \(\bar k\)-rational (the residue field of a closed point of a curve over
486	  \(\bar k\) is a finite extension of \(\bar k\), hence is \(\bar k\) itself by
487	  algebraic closedness). Fix one such \(\bar k\)-rational point \(P \in C\);
488	  this is the marked point used to build the linear system below.
489	  \emph{Step 1: non-constant function \(f \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))\).}
490	  By Hartshorne's Riemann--Roch in genus zero
491	  (\texttt{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero} of \texttt{RR.2}) applied to the
492	  effective divisor \([P]\) of degree \(1\), we obtain
493	  \(\ell([P]) = \deg([P]) + 1 = 2\). The constant section
494	  \(1 \in \bar k \cdot 1 \subset H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))\) accounts for a
495	  one-dimensional \(\bar k\)-subspace; the two-dimensional quotient supplies
496	  a non-constant section $f \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P])) \setminus
497	  \bar k \cdot 1$. This is the corollary
498	  \texttt{cor:nonconstant\_function\_genus\_zero} of \texttt{RR.3} (sibling
499	  chapter, \texttt{RiemannRoch\_OCofP.tex}).
500	  \emph{Step 2: morphism \(C \to \mathbb P^1\) via the linear system \((1, f)\).}
501	  The pair \((1, f) \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))^2\) generates the
502	  invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) at every point of \(C\): at points
503	  \(Q \in C \setminus \{P\}\) the section \(1\) does not vanish in
504	  \(\mathcal O_C([P])_Q \cong \mathcal O_{C, Q}\), and at the marked point
505	  \(P\) the section \(f\) does not vanish in \(\mathcal O_C([P])_P\) (because \(f\)
506	  has a pole of order exactly \(1\) at \(P\) in the rational-function
507	  picture: \(f \in K(C) \setminus \bar k\) with \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty = [P]\)).
508	  Therefore by \texttt{lem:morphism_to_p1_from_global_sections} there
509	  exists a unique morphism
510	  \[
511	    \varphi \;:=\; \varphi_{(1, f)} \colon C \longrightarrow \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}
512	  \]
513	  with \(\varphi^* x_0 = 1\) and \(\varphi^* x_1 = f\) (so the pullback of
514	  the standard ratio coordinate \(u = x_1 / x_0\) on $\mathbb P^1 \setminus
515	  \{\infty\}\( is \)f$, and the pullback of the inverse coordinate
516	  \(t_\infty = x_0 / x_1 = 1 / f\) is the local parameter \(1/f\) at \(P\)).
517	  \emph{Step 3: \(\deg(\varphi) = 1\) via the pole divisor of \(f\).} The
518	  morphism \(\varphi\) is non-constant (since \(f\) is non-constant as a
519	  rational function on \(C\), the pullback \(\varphi^* u = f\) takes
520	  infinitely many values, ruling out a constant \(\varphi\)). By
521	  \texttt{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor} applied to \(\varphi\),
522	  \[
523	    \deg(\varphi)
524	    \;=\;
525	    \deg\!\left(\,\sum_{Q \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)}
526	                  \ord_Q(\varphi^* t_\infty) \cdot [Q]\,\right).
527	  \]
528	  We compute the pole divisor of \(f\) as a rational function on \(C\):
529	  since \(f \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))\) with \(f\) non-constant and
530	  \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) the line bundle of the effective divisor \([P]\), the
531	  rational-function \(f\) has pole divisor \((f)_\infty = [P]\) (the
532	  unique pole of order \(1\) at \(P\), no other poles; this is the standard
533	  identification of \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))\) with the rational functions
534	  \(g \in K(C)\) satisfying \(\mathrm{div}(g) + D \geq 0\), specialised to
535	  \(D = [P]\)). Hence \(\varphi^{-1}(\infty) = \{P\}\) with multiplicity~\(1\)
536	  and
537	  \[
538	    \deg(\varphi) \;=\; \deg([P]) \;=\; 1.
539	  \]
540	  \emph{Step 4: \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism.} By Hartshorne's IV.2
541	  identification \(\deg(\varphi) = [K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)]\), the
542	  degree-one conclusion of Step~3 rewrites as
543	  \(\operatorname{Module.finrank}_{\bar k(\mathbb P^1)} K(C) = 1\) (with
544	  the canonical \(\varphi\)-induced
545	  \texttt{[Algebra \,(ProjectiveLineBar kbar).functionField\,
546	   C.functionField]} instance). By
547	  \texttt{lem:degree_one_morphism_iso} applied to the non-constant
548	  morphism \(\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) (smooth proper
549	  geometrically irreducible curves over \(\bar k\) on both sides), under
550	  this function-field-extension-degree-one hypothesis, \(\varphi\) is an
551	  isomorphism of \(\bar k\)-schemes.
552	  This produces the required isomorphism \(C \cong \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\)
553	  closing the headline target
554	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}.
555	  \emph{Comparison with Hartshorne's original argument.} Hartshorne's
556	  Example~IV.1.3.5 phrases the argument in terms of two distinct points
557	  \(P, Q \in C\) and the degree-zero divisor \(D = P - Q\), deriving \(P \sim Q\)
558	  and concluding via II.6.10.1 that \(C\) is rational; combined with I.6.12
559	  this yields \(C \cong \mathbb P^1\). The present proof restates the same
560	  combinatorial content via the more directly formalisable linear-system
561	  construction: instead of going through the \(P \sim Q\) statement and
562	  invoking II.6.10.1 as a black box, we produce the explicit morphism
563	  \(\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1\) from the two sections \((1, f)\) of
564	  \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) given by Riemann--Roch and identify its degree
565	  directly. The two arguments are mathematically equivalent and have the
566	  same dependency footprint (Riemann--Roch in genus zero \(\Rightarrow\)
567	  \(\ell([P]) = 2\) \(\Rightarrow\) non-constant \(f\) \(\Rightarrow\) degree-\(1\)
568	  map to \(\mathbb P^1\) \(\Rightarrow\) iso); the linear-system phrasing maps
569	  more cleanly onto the Mathlib \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} API and
570	  the project's existing Weil-divisor/degree pipeline of \texttt{RR.1}.
571	\end{proof}

647	/-- **Degree of a non-constant morphism to `ℙ¹` via its pole divisor**
648	(Hartshorne IV §2 p. 299 opening + II §6.9 multiplicativity of degree
649	under finite pullback).
650	
651	For a non-constant morphism `φ : C ⟶ ProjectiveLineBar k̄` from a smooth
652	proper geometrically irreducible curve `C` over an algebraically closed
653	field `k̄`, there exists a positive integer `d` (= `deg(φ)`) together
654	with a Weil divisor `D` on `C` (= the pole divisor `φ^*[∞]` of the
655	pulled-back affine coordinate) of total degree `d`.
656	
657	The non-constancy hypothesis is encoded as `∀ Q, φ ≠ toUnit C ≫ Q`
658	(no constant morphism `C ⟶ ℙ¹` agrees with `φ`); this is equivalent to
659	`φ` being surjective onto `ℙ¹_{k̄}` since the image is a closed
660	irreducible subset of `ℙ¹_{k̄}` of dimension ≥ 1, and the only such is
661	`ℙ¹_{k̄}` itself.

663	iter-178+ body: a non-constant morphism between smooth proper curves
664	over `k̄` is automatically finite (proper + quasi-finite). The
665	function-field extension `k̄(ℙ¹) ↪ K(C)` is finite of some degree
666	`d = [K(C) : k̄(ℙ¹)] ≥ 1`. Take `D := φ^*[∞] ∈ Div(C)` via the standard

668	Hartshorne II.6.9, `deg(D) = deg(φ) · deg([∞]) = d · 1 = d`. The

\section{A degree-\(1\) morphism between smooth proper curves is an isomorphism}
input.new_string:
\end{proof}
\subsection{The pole divisor \(\varphi^*[\infty]\) and its degree (Lean substrate)}
% The four blocks of this subsection give 1-to-1 blueprint coverage for the
% Lean helper declarations underlying `lem:degree_via_pole_divisor` above
% (the local parameter at \(\infty\), its uniformiser property, the pole
% divisor itself, and the function-field-degree identity). They are
% project-internal plumbing; the external grounding is the Hartshorne IV.2 /
% II.6.9 material already cited on `lem:degree_via_pole_divisor`, so no
% further verbatim citation is attached here.
\begin{definition}[Local parameter at \(\infty \in \mathbb P^1\)]
  \label{def:localParameterAtInfty}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.localParameterAtInfty}
  \uses{def:functionFieldIso}
  Let \(\bar k\) be a field and present the projective line as
  \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k} = \operatorname{Proj} \bar k[X_0, X_1]\). The
  \emph{local parameter at \(\infty\)} is the rational function
  \[
    t_\infty \;:=\; X_0 / X_1 \;\in\; K(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}),
  \]
  the degree-\(0\) ratio coordinate of the homogeneous localisation
  \(\bigl(\bar k[X_0, X_1]_{(X_1)}\bigr)_0 = \mathcal O\!\bigl(D_+(X_1)\bigr)\),
  transported into the function field \(K(\mathbb P^1)\) along the germ map
  on the affine chart \(D_+(X_1)\). On the Lean side it is packaged as a
  \emph{nonzero} element of \(K(\mathbb P^1)\): the witness \(t_\infty \neq 0\)
  comes from the injectivity of the chart isomorphism
  \(\mathcal O\!\bigl(D_+(X_1)\bigr) \cong \bar k[X_0/X_1]\) (a
  \(\operatorname{Proj}\)-chart presentation) composed with the injective
  germ-to-function-field map of the integral scheme \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\),
  applied to the nonzero ratio element \(X_0 / X_1\).
\end{definition}
\begin{proof}
  Proved directly in Lean.
\end{proof}
\begin{lemma}[\(t_\infty\) is a uniformiser at \([\infty]\)]
  \label{lem:localParameterAtInfty_uniformiser_witness}
  \uses{def:localParameterAtInfty}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.localParameterAtInfty_uniformiser_witness}
  There is a unique prime divisor \(Y_0 \in \Div(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k})\) — the
  point at infinity \([\infty]\) — at which the local parameter
  \(t_\infty = X_0 / X_1\) of \cref{def:localParameterAtInfty} has positive
  order, and there \(\ord_{Y_0}(t_\infty) = 1\). Equivalently, the zero divisor
  \((t_\infty)_0 = [\infty]\) of \(t_\infty\) on \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) has
  degree \(1\) and is supported at the single prime divisor \([\infty]\).
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:localParameterAtInfty}
  \emph{Honest sketch (the Lean body is an open hole).} The witness \(Y_0\) is
  the closed point at infinity, image under \(\operatorname{Proj}.\mathrm{aw\!ay\iota}\)
  at \(X_1\) of the maximal ideal generated by \(X_0/X_1\) in the affine chart
  \(\operatorname{Spec} \bar k[X_0/X_1]\). On that chart the stalk at \(Y_0\) is
  the discrete valuation ring \(\bar k[X_0/X_1]_{(X_0/X_1)}\) with uniformiser
  \(X_0/X_1 = t_\infty\), so \(\ord_{Y_0}(t_\infty) = 1\); and on the
  complementary chart \(D_+(X_0)\) the function \(t_\infty\) is a unit, whence
  no other prime divisor carries positive order. Formalising this requires the
  not-yet-available stalk-of-\(\operatorname{Proj}\)-chart-is-a-DVR bridge and
  the order-via-uniformiser identity (the same codimension-one regularity
  substrate that \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\)'s
  \texttt{IsRegularInCodimensionOne} instance is gated on); pending that, the
  Lean proof is left as an explicit hole.
\end{proof}
\begin{definition}[Pole divisor of \(\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1\)]
  \label{def:hom_poleDivisor}
  \uses{def:localParameterAtInfty, def:WeilDivisor_positivePart, def:principal_divisor}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor}
  Let \(C / \bar k\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve, regular
  in codimension one, and let \(\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) be a
  morphism, with \(K(C)\) carrying the canonical \(\varphi\)-induced
  \(K(\mathbb P^1)\)-algebra structure. The \emph{pole divisor} of \(\varphi\)
  is the Weil divisor
  \[
    \varphi^*[\infty]
    \;:=\;
    \bigl(\operatorname{div}(\varphi^\# t_\infty)\bigr)_0
    \;=\;
    \operatorname{positivePart}\!\bigl(\operatorname{div}(\varphi^\# t_\infty)\bigr)
    \;\in\; \Div(C),
  \]
  the positive part of the principal divisor on \(C\) of the pulled-back local
  parameter \(\varphi^\# t_\infty = \operatorname{algebraMap}_{K(\mathbb P^1) \to K(C)}(t_\infty)\),
  where \(t_\infty\) is the local parameter of \cref{def:localParameterAtInfty}.
  The pullback is nonzero because the algebra map \(K(\mathbb P^1) \to K(C)\) is
  injective (a ring homomorphism out of a field), so \(t_\infty \neq 0\) gives
  \(\varphi^\# t_\infty \neq 0\) and the principal divisor is well defined. On a
  complete nonsingular curve the zeros of \(\varphi^\# t_\infty\) are exactly the
  preimages of \(\infty\), so the positive part recovers \(\varphi^*[\infty]\) on
  the nose.
\end{definition}
\begin{proof}
  Proved directly in Lean.
\end{proof}
\begin{theorem}[Degree of the pole divisor equals the function-field degree]
  \label{thm:poleDivisor_degree_eq_finrank}
  \uses{def:hom_poleDivisor, lem:localParameterAtInfty_uniformiser_witness,
        lem:degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank, def:divisor_degree}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor_degree_eq_finrank}
  For a non-constant morphism \(\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) of
  smooth proper geometrically irreducible curves over \(\bar k\) (with \(K(C)\)
  finite over \(K(\mathbb P^1)\) via the \(\varphi\)-induced algebra structure),
  the degree of the pole divisor equals the function-field extension degree:
  \[
    \deg\!\bigl(\varphi^*[\infty]\bigr)
    \;=\;
    [\,K(C) : K(\mathbb P^1)\,]
    \;=\;
    \operatorname{finrank}_{K(\mathbb P^1)} K(C).
  \]
  This is the precise form of the degree identity packaged existentially by
  \cref{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor}.
\end{theorem}
\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:hom_poleDivisor, lem:localParameterAtInfty_uniformiser_witness,
        lem:degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank}
  Proved directly in Lean: unfolding \(\varphi^*[\infty] =
  \operatorname{positivePart}(\operatorname{div}(\varphi^\# t_\infty))\)
  reduces the claim to the Hartshorne~II.6.9 degree identity
  \cref{lem:degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank} of \texttt{RR.1},
  specialised at the local parameter \(t = t_\infty\), whose uniformiser
  hypothesis is supplied by \cref{lem:localParameterAtInfty_uniformiser_witness}.
\end{proof}
\section{A degree-\(1\) morphism between smooth proper curves is an isomorphism}
